text: The surgeon adjusts trocar placement to optimize working space and prevent robotic arm collision. The stomach is lifted and secured, while the omentum is divided along its greater curvature. The anterior surface of the pancreas is exposed, and dissection proceeds to the right. The right colon is detached and lowered, and the duodenum is mobilized, revealing the vena cava.

The surgeon performs dissection of lymph nodes along the hepatic artery. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected, encircled, and divided between two clips following a clamping test. The common bile duct is dissected, clamped with a bulldog clamp, and divided. Pilar lymphadenectomy is completed. The gastroepiploic vessels are controlled and divided. The duodenum is divided with a stapler, two centimeters below the pylorus.

The surgeon continues dissection along the portal vein, passing an umbilical tape around the pancreatic neck. The superior mesenteric vein is carefully detached from the uncinate process, with small venous branches controlled using clips. The uncinate process of the pancreas is mobilized, and the pancreas is divided using harmonic shears until the pancreatic duct is identified. The pancreatic duct is then divided with scissors, leaving an adequate stump for future anastomosis, completing the pancreatic transection.

The surgeon mobilizes the ligament of Treitz and passes the jejunum behind the mesenteric vessels. A stapler is used to transect the bowel. The pancreatic head is lifted, and the uncinate process is dissected from the portal vein and superior mesenteric artery. The inferior pancreatic artery is identified and divided between clips. The portal vein and superior mesenteric artery are completely skeletonized, marking the completion of the pancreaticoduodenectomy.

The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunostomy using a duct-to-mucosa technique. The posterior layer is sutured with interrupted 4-0 Prolene stitches, followed by a duct-to-mucosa anastomosis using interrupted V-O sutures. A pediatric feeding tube is placed as an internal stent, and the anterior part of the anastomosis is completed.

The surgeon removes the bulldog clamp from the common bile duct and creates an opening in the jejunum using harmonic shears. An end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy is performed with a continuous suture, showing no bile leakage upon testing. An anticholic duodenogastrojejunostomy is carried out, with the posterior layer sutured using a running suture and the anterior layer with interrupted sutures. The stomach and jejunum are opened, and a full-thickness anastomosis is completed with absorbable sutures. The surgical specimen is retrieved through the umbilical port after incision enlargement, and frozen section analysis confirms free surgical margins. The distal bile duct with a tumor is visible.
